unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to your first unit of Year 2 English. You will learn how to say and spell your name, introduce a partner, and say what people like using like and likes. You will learn the calendar words too: the days of the week, the months of the year and the order words we use for dates. You will hunt for English words around your home, and ask simple questions about colours and numbers. By the end you will be ready to welcome a new friend, just as Amal does in this unit's story, “Amal's First Week”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few vocabulary words each day and say every example sentence aloud.
Work through the six grammar practices: he and she, like and likes, and asking questions.
Read “Words Around Us”, the poem, the two listening texts and the story “Amal's First Week”, then answer the seventeen comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The word that people call you when they talk to you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My name is Amal, and I write it on my book.
practiceSentences: Amal writes her name at the top of the page.

readings.title: Words Around Us
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Words are all around us. We can see words in books. We can see words on tablets. We can see words on signs. We can see words at home. Words help us learn!

readings.title: A Poem: “When I Open Up a Book”
readings.passageScript:
When I open up a book
Each page whispers, “Look! Look! Look!”
Sports and monkeys, trains and kings,
Stories of amazing things.

readings.title: Meeting a Partner
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Maya introduce her partner. Ask an adult to read it aloud, then read it yourself.
“This is my partner. Her name is Nora. She likes trees. She is my friend.”

readings.title: The Days of the Week Song
readings.passageScript:
Sing along:
“Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday. Seven days make one week — seven days in all!”

readings.title: Amal's First Week
readings.theme: Meeting new friends, saying your name, and learning the days of the week.
readings.setting: A primary school near the market in Amal's town.
readings.passageScript:
It was Amal's first day at a new school. The school was near the market in her town. Amal held her mother's hand and looked at the big blue door.
“Good morning,” said a kind teacher. “Welcome! My name is Teacher Yasmin. What is your name?”
“My name is Amal,” she said in a small voice.
“Can you spell it for me, Amal?” asked Teacher Yasmin.
“A-M-A-L,” said Amal. She felt a little braver now.
Inside the classroom, the children sat in a circle. On the wall there was a big calendar and a chart of colours. A boy with a yellow cap smiled at Amal.
“Hello! My name is Leo,” he said. “Today is Saturday. At our school, Saturday is the first day of the week. You can be my partner!”
“Thank you, Leo,” said Amal. She liked him already.
Teacher Yasmin pointed to the calendar. “Let us say the days of the week together,” she said. The whole class sang: “Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday!”
“Now,” said Teacher Yasmin, “tell your partner one thing you like.”
Amal turned to Leo. “I like drawing,” she said. “What do you like?”
“I like football,” said Leo. “And I like mangoes! My birthday is in the month of June, when the mangoes are sweet.”
Amal laughed. “My birthday is in December,” she said. “I will be seven years old.”
All week, Amal learned something new. On Monday she learned her colours. On Tuesday she counted to twelve. On Wednesday she read her first English book, about a clever fox.
On Thursday, a new girl came to the class. She stood by the door and looked shy, just like Amal on her first day.
Amal remembered how kind Leo had been. She walked over and smiled.
“Hello,” said Amal. “My name is Amal. What is your name? You can be my partner today.”
The new girl smiled back. “My name is Nora,” she said. “Thank you.”
Teacher Yasmin watched and nodded. “Well done, Amal,” she said softly. “A good friend makes everybody feel welcome.”
That night, Amal told her mother about her week. “I have two friends now,” she said happily. “Leo and Nora. And I know all the days of the week!”
Her mother hugged her. “You had a wonderful first week,” she said. “Tomorrow is Friday, a day to rest.”
Amal closed her eyes and smiled. She could not wait for Saturday, when a brand new school week would begin.

comprehension.question: The text names four places where we can see words. What are they?
comprehension.correctAnswer: In books, on tablets, on signs and at home.
comprehension.explanation: The four places are listed in the four middle sentences, one place in each sentence.

comprehension.question: The last line of the text tells us why words matter. What does it say that words do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It says that words help us learn.
comprehension.explanation: The final short sentence, “Words help us learn!”, gives the reason.

comprehension.question: Look around your own home. Name two places where you can see words that this text does not name.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept any two real places that are not in the text, such as on a food packet, a shop sign, a shirt label or a television screen.
comprehension.explanation: The child must first notice which four places the text already names, then add new ones of their own.

comprehension.question: In the poem, what does each page whisper when you open a book?
comprehension.explanation: The second line of the poem gives the exact words that the page whispers.

comprehension.question: The third line of the poem lists four things you can read about. Name them, then say which one you would choose.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Sports, monkeys, trains and kings. Then accept any one of the four with a reason for the choice.
comprehension.explanation: The first part is straight recall from line three. The second part asks the child to say what they like and why.

comprehension.question: Who is Maya's partner, and what does that partner like?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Maya's partner is Nora, and Nora likes trees.
comprehension.explanation: Maya gives her partner's name in the second sentence and says what she likes in the third.

comprehension.question: Does Maya say “he” or “she” when she talks about Nora? Why does she choose that word?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She says “she”, because Nora is a girl. She also uses “her” when she says “Her name is Nora”.
comprehension.explanation: This links the listening text to Grammar practice 1: she is used for a girl or a woman.

comprehension.question: How many days does the song say make one week?
comprehension.explanation: The last line of the song answers the question twice: “Seven days make one week — seven days in all!”

comprehension.question: Which day does the song sing straight after Wednesday?
comprehension.explanation: The song lists the days in order, so the child can find Wednesday and read on by one day.

comprehension.question: How does Amal spell her name for Teacher Yasmin?
comprehension.explanation: Amal spells her name aloud near the start of the story, and it makes her feel braver.

comprehension.question: Who is Amal's first partner, and what does he like?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Leo is her first partner. He likes football and mangoes.
comprehension.explanation: The boy with the yellow cap gives his name, and later tells Amal two things that he likes.

comprehension.question: Which day is the first day of the school week in this story?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Saturday is the first day of the school week.
comprehension.explanation: Leo says it on Amal's first morning, and the class then sings the days in that order.

comprehension.question: In which month is Leo's birthday, and in which month is Amal's?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Leo's birthday is in June and Amal's birthday is in December.
comprehension.explanation: Leo links his birthday to sweet mangoes in June, and Amal answers with her own month.

comprehension.question: What kind thing did Amal do for Nora on Thursday, and why did she do it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She said hello and asked Nora to be her partner, because she remembered how kind Leo had been to her.
comprehension.explanation: The story says that Amal remembered Leo's kindness first, and then walked over to the new girl.

comprehension.question: How did Amal feel on her first morning, and how had she changed by Thursday? Use the story to explain.
comprehension.correctAnswer: At first she spoke in a small voice and felt shy. By Thursday she felt brave enough to welcome a new girl. Two new friends and a kind teacher helped her to change.
comprehension.explanation: Look for evidence from the story: the small voice at the start, and the welcome that Amal gives on Thursday.

comprehension.question: Teacher Yasmin said, “A good friend makes everybody feel welcome.” What is one thing you could say or do to welcome a new child?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept any kind, practical idea, such as saying hello, asking the child's name, offering to be their partner or sharing a book.
comprehension.explanation: A strong answer names one action and says how it would help the new child to feel welcome.

comprehension.question: Amal learned something new on each day of her week. Which day of your own week do you enjoy most, and what do you do on that day?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept any day named correctly, with one activity that really happens on it. The day name should be written with a capital letter.
comprehension.explanation: This links the story week to the child's own week and practises the day names in a real sentence.

grammar.title: He and She: Choose the Pronoun
grammar.explanation: We use he and she so that we do not need to say a person's name again and again. Use he for a boy or a man, and she for a girl or a woman.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use he for a boy or a man: This is Leo. He likes football.
Use she for a girl or a woman: This is Nora. She likes reading.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write, “This is my sister. He likes drawing.” A sister is a girl, so the second sentence needs she: “This is my sister. She likes drawing.”
grammar.memoryTip: She and sister begin with the same s sound. If the person is a girl or a woman, choose the word that starts with s.
grammar.practice:
Write he or she in each gap.
1. This is my brother. ____ likes running.
2. This is Nora. ____ likes singing.
3. This is my father. ____ works in the market.
4. This is Maya. ____ is my best friend.

grammar.title: He and She: Introduce a Person
grammar.explanation: Now you introduce somebody yourself. Say the person's name in the first sentence, then use he or she in the sentences that follow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
This is Leo. He is my partner. He likes football.
This is Amal. She is my friend. She likes drawing.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to swap words halfway, as in “This is Nora. He likes trees.” Keep the same pronoun for the same person all the way through.
grammar.memoryTip: Name first, pronoun next. Once you have chosen he or she for a person, keep it for that person.
grammar.practice: Choose one boy or man and one girl or woman that you know. Write two sentences about each person. Use the name in the first sentence and he or she in the second.

grammar.title: Like and Likes: Choose the Form
grammar.explanation: We use like and likes to say what somebody enjoys. The form you choose depends on the person you are talking about.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use like with I, you, we and they.
I like bananas. We like singing. They like playing outside.
Use likes with he, she and it.
He likes football. She likes drawing. It likes water.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “She like mangoes.” leaves off the s. He, she and it always need the s, so the sentence becomes “She likes mangoes.”
grammar.memoryTip: He, she and it are the three little words that add the s. Say the rhyme: “He, she, it — do not forget the s on it!”
grammar.practice:
Write like or likes in each gap.
1. I ____ football.
2. My mother ____ tea.
3. They ____ the calendar song.
4. He ____ funny stories.
5. We ____ our new school.

grammar.title: Like and Likes: Class Survey
grammar.explanation: In a survey you ask other people a question and write down what they say. When you report one person's answer, remember to add the s to likes.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Ask: What do you like?
Answer: I like drawing.
Report: Nora likes drawing.
Ask: What does Leo like? Report: He likes football.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often keep the answer's form when they report it, as in “Nora like drawing.” The report is about one other person, so it needs likes.
grammar.memoryTip: Ask, listen, then report. The moment you talk about one other person, the s comes back.
grammar.practice: Ask three people at home what they like. Write each answer in two ways: first as the person said it, then as a report. For example: “I like tea.” becomes “My mother likes tea.”

grammar.title: Asking Questions: Build the Question
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions. This unit uses three question patterns: one for spelling, one for colours and one for how many.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
How do you spell ...? becomes How do you spell your name?
What colour is ...? becomes What colour is your bag? It is blue.
How many ... are there? becomes How many chairs are there? There are six chairs.
grammar.commonMistake: After how many, most naming words need an s at the end. “How many chair are there?” becomes “How many chairs are there?”
grammar.memoryTip: Question word first, question mark last. Every question you write needs both of them.
grammar.practice:
Write the question that fits each answer.
1. ______? “My name is spelled A-M-A-L.”
2. ______? “My bag is green.”
3. ______? “There are twelve months.”

grammar.title: Asking Questions: Ask and Answer
grammar.explanation: A good answer matches the question. A colour question needs a colour in the answer, and a how many question needs a number.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What colour is the calendar? The calendar is blue.
How many books are there? There are four books.
How do you spell Nora? It is spelled N-O-R-A.
grammar.commonMistake: An answer such as “It is big.” does not match the question “What colour is your bag?” Listen for the question word before you answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Match the answer to the question word: a colour word for colour, a number word for how many, and letters for spelling.
grammar.practice: Work with an adult and take turns to ask six questions: two about spelling, two about colours and two about numbers. Write every question and every answer as a full sentence.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Introduce Yourself
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say three sentences about yourself.
“My name is ______.”
“I am ______ years old.”
“I like ______.”
Say them to an adult first, then record yourself. Check: did you say your name clearly, did you say what you like, and did you speak loudly enough?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Say your three sentences to the tutor while your helper listens, and ask it which sentence was the clearest.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Talk About Your Birthday
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Talk about your birthday in two sentences.
“My birthday is on the ______ of ______.”
“On my birthday, I like to ______.”
Say the order word first and the month afterwards, and say the date slowly.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor your birthday date with an adult beside you, and ask it to repeat the date so that you can check the order word.

speaking.title: Speaking Game — I Spy
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this colour game with an adult or a friend.
Choose an object that you can both see and say: “I spy something ______.” Name the colour in the gap.
The other person guesses: “Is it the ______?”
Answer “Yes, it is!” or “No, it is not.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to play three rounds of I Spy with you while your helper sits beside you and keeps the score.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Spell Your Name
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Work with a partner and take turns.
Ask: “What is your name? How do you spell it?”
Answer: “My name is Amal. I spell it A-M-A-L.”
Say one letter at a time and leave a small gap between the letters. Then swap roles and record your best turn.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Spell your name letter by letter for the tutor, with your helper listening, and ask whether every letter was clear.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Calendar Chant
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Say the seven days of the week in order, then say the twelve months in order.
Start slowly, then say them again at a steady pace.
Finish by saying today's date in a full sentence, such as “Today is Monday, the fifth of May.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Chant the days and the months to the tutor while an adult listens, and ask it to name any day or month that you missed.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Welcome a New Friend
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Role-play meeting a new classmate, just as Amal met Nora.
Say: “Hello. My name is ______. What is your name? You can be my partner.”
Then ask one question about what the new child likes, and answer the same question yourself.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to play the new child while your helper watches, then ask for one idea that would make your welcome even kinder.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Simple Sentences About You
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these four sentences about yourself and your friend, then write two more sentences of your own.
My name is ______.
I like ______.
My friend's name is ______.
He or she likes ______.
writing.modelText: My name is Sami. I like football. My friend's name is Nora. She likes drawing. We sit together at the blue table. On Saturday we play outside.
writing.successCriteria: I used a capital letter for my name and for my friend's name, chose he or she correctly, and ended every sentence with a full stop.
writing.support: Say each sentence aloud before you write it, and copy the words name, like and friend from your word wall.
writing.extension: Join two of your sentences with and, such as “I like drawing and I like football.”

writing.title: Writing 2 — Your Own Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write your own version of the poem “When I Open Up a Book”. Keep the first two lines and the last line, and change the third line to the things that you like to read about. Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText:
When I open up a book
Each page whispers, “Look! Look! Look!”
Trains and planets, cats and kings,
Stories of amazing things.
writing.sentenceStarter: When I open up a book
writing.successCriteria: My third line names four things that I like to read about, my poem keeps the shape of the original, and my picture matches my words.
writing.support: Make a list of six things that you like to read about, then choose the four that sound best together.
writing.extension: Try to make your third line rhyme with the last line, as the original poem does.

writing.title: Story response: Amal's First Week
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three sentences about your own first week at a school or a club. Begin the first sentence with “On Monday I ______.” Then draw Amal, Leo and Nora, and write one sentence under your picture about what each person likes.
writing.modelText:
On Monday I felt shy in my new class. On Tuesday a girl asked me to be her partner. By Friday I had two new friends.
Amal likes drawing. Leo likes football. Nora likes trees.
writing.successCriteria: My three sentences are in day order, and each caption uses likes because it tells about one person.
writing.support: Look back at the story to check what each character likes before you write your captions.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that says how you felt at the end of your week and why you felt that way.

writing.title: Writing 4 — My Special Date
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write four sentences about one date that is special to you. Name the day, write the date with its order word, name the month, and say why the date is special.
writing.modelText: My special date is Saturday, the third of August. It is my birthday. My family cooks my favourite meal. I feel happy on this day.
writing.successCriteria: I wrote the day and the month with capital letters, used the correct order word for the date, and gave one reason why the date is special.
writing.support: Find the date on a calendar first, and point to it while you say your sentence aloud.
writing.extension: Add a fifth sentence that uses because, such as “I like this date because my cousins visit.”

writing.title: Writing 5 — Welcome Card
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make a welcome card for a new child in your class. Inside the card, greet the child, introduce yourself, say one thing you like, ask one question and offer one kind thing that you will do.
writing.modelText: Hello Nora! My name is Maya. I like reading. What do you like? You can sit next to me today. Welcome to our class!
writing.successCriteria: My card greets the new child by name, asks one question with a question mark, and offers one kind action.
writing.support: Use the words hello, name, like, partner and welcome from your word wall.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that tells the new child something useful about your classroom.

writing.title: Writing 6 — My Two-Week Learning Diary
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write one sentence about something you learned in each of the six live sessions, in order. Finish with one sentence that names a goal for your next English unit.
writing.modelText: In Session 1, I said my name and spelled it. In Session 2, I read about Amal's first week. In Session 3, I sorted he and she. In Session 4, I chanted the months. In Session 5, I wrote my special date. In Session 6, I shared my welcome card. Next, I want to read a whole page on my own.
writing.successCriteria: My seven sentences follow the order of the sessions, each one names something that I really did, and the last one names a goal.
writing.support: Look at your saved recordings and writing before you start, and use the session titles as reminders.
writing.extension: Underline the session that helped you most, then write one sentence that explains why.

activities.title: Activity 1 — True or False (Calendar Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
1. There are 12 months in a year.
2. There are 8 days in a week.
3. January is the first month of the year.
4. September has 31 days.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True. 2. False, there are 7 days in a week. 3. True. 4. False, September has 30 days.

activities.title: Activity 2 — Match the Months to the Season
activities.instructionsAndItems: Where you live, which months are hot and dry, and which months bring rain? Make two lists with the month names in order, then check them with an adult, because the seasons are different in different places.
activities.answerSummary: The lists depend on where the child lives. Check that every month appears once, that each one starts with a capital letter, and that the two lists do not disagree with each other.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Copy each sentence and fill the gap.
1. Today is ______.
2. My birthday is in the month of ______.
3. The first day of my school week is ______.
4. There are ______ months in a year.
activities.answerSummary: 1. The day on which the child is working, with a capital letter. 2. Any month, with a capital letter. 3. The first school day where the child lives; in the story it is Saturday. 4. Twelve.

activities.title: Activity 4 — Put the Days in Order
activities.instructionsAndItems: Put these days in the school-week order used in the story: Wednesday | Saturday | Monday | Friday | Tuesday | Thursday | Sunday.
activities.answerSummary: Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday.

activities.title: Activity 5 — Month Detective
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find and write the answer to each clue: the first month of the year; the sixth month; the month that comes after August; the month that comes before December; the month of your own birthday.
activities.answerSummary: January; June; September; November; and the child's own birthday month, spelled with a capital letter.

activities.title: Activity 6 — Colour and Number Hunt
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find three groups of objects at home. For each group, ask and answer two questions: “What colour is it?” and “How many can you see?” Write every answer as a full sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Three pairs of sentences, such as “The cups are white.” and “I can see four cups.” Check the colour word, the number word and the full stop.

assignments.title: Welcome and Calendar learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work. First finish “Writing 1 — Simple Sentences About You” and check every capital letter. Next record “Speaking 1 — Introduce Yourself”, saying your name, your age and one thing you like. Finally finish “Writing 4 — My Special Date” and write the date with the correct order word. Check all three against the writing and speaking rubrics before you send them together.

quizzes.question: What does the word name mean?
quizzes.options: A person you work or play with. | The word that people call you. | A chart that shows the months. | To enjoy something very much.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The word that people call you.
quizzes.explanation: A name is what people call you. The other three choices describe a partner, a calendar and the verb like.

quizzes.question: Can you ____ your name for me, please?
quizzes.explanation: Spell means to say or write the letters in order. Brown is a colour, eleventh is an order word and a calendar is a chart.

quizzes.question: Which word means a person you work or play with?
quizzes.explanation: A partner works or plays with you. A picture is a drawing, a week is seven days and yellow is a colour.

quizzes.question: This is Nora. ____ likes singing.
quizzes.explanation: Nora is one girl, so we use she. He is for a boy, and they and we are used for more than one person.

quizzes.question: Leo ____ football and mangoes.
quizzes.explanation: Leo is one person, just like he, so the verb needs the s. Like belongs with I, you, we and they.

quizzes.question: Which day comes straight after Wednesday?
quizzes.explanation: The order in the song is Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday. Monday, Friday and Tuesday sit in other places in the week.

quizzes.question: How many months are there in one year?
quizzes.explanation: A year has twelve months. Seven is the number of days in a week, thirty is the number of days in many months, and ten is the number of your fingers.

quizzes.question: Which word is the order word for number 3?
quizzes.explanation: Third tells you the position in a line or on a calendar. Three and thirty are counting numbers, and Thursday is a day.

quizzes.question: In the story, what did Amal do when Nora stood shyly by the door?
quizzes.options: She asked Nora to be her partner. | She read the calendar to the class. | She drew a picture of a clever fox. | She waved goodbye and went home.
quizzes.correctAnswer: She asked Nora to be her partner.
quizzes.explanation: Amal remembered how kind Leo had been on her own first day, so she welcomed Nora and offered to be her partner.

quizzes.question: Which one is a complete question about colour?
quizzes.options: What colour is your bag? | Your bag colour is. | Colour bag what is? | How colour your bag.
quizzes.explanation: The pattern is “What colour is ...?” with a question mark at the end. The other three do not use the question words in the right order.

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes that an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The tutor prompts from the source pack are grouped here by file. Lesson: “Can you read the poem with me and explain it?”, “Can you play a listening game about the days of the week?”, “Can you help me practise introducing myself?”, “Can you play I Spy with me?”, “How do I say the date of my birthday correctly?”, “Can you check if my sentences are correct?”, “Can you help me make my poem rhyme?” Story: “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?”, “Can you check my sentences about my school week?”, “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “Why do we say he likes but I like?”, “Can you give me five more sentences to practise he and she?” Vocabulary: “How do I spell this word?”, “Can you quiz me on the days of the week and the months?”, “Can you help me read this word that I found?”, “Can you quiz me on colour words?”, “Can you help me count to twelve in English?”

This is the first unit of the year, so spend the first session learning names and making every child feel welcome, exactly as the story models. Children who are new to English can join every speaking task by pointing, copying and chanting before they answer alone. In the story the school week begins on Saturday; ask the class which day begins their own week and accept their answer in Activity 3, because school weeks differ from place to place. When marking, treat a missing s on likes as the next step rather than as a mistake, and praise correct capital letters on names, days and months. Keep a calendar and a colour chart on display all fortnight so that children can point to the words they are learning.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. True, a year has twelve months. 2. False, there are seven days in a week. 3. True, January comes first. 4. False, September has thirty days.
1. He (a brother is a boy). 2. She (Nora is a girl). 3. He (a father is a man). 4. She (Maya is a girl).
1. like. 2. likes. 3. like. 4. likes. 5. like. The s appears only after my mother and he.
1. How do you spell your name? 2. What colour is your bag? 3. How many months are there? Every answer needs a question mark.
In books, on tablets, on signs and at home. All four places are needed for the mark.
Words help us learn. Accept any wording that gives the same reason.
Any two real places that the text does not name, for example a food packet, a shop sign, a shirt label or a screen. Do not accept books, tablets, signs or home again.
Each page whispers, “Look! Look! Look!” Accept the words without the exclamation marks.
Sports, monkeys, trains and kings. Award the second mark when the child chooses one of the four and gives a reason.
Maya's partner is Nora, and Nora likes trees. Both halves are needed.
She, because Nora is a girl. Accept any answer that names the reason, including the use of her name.
Seven. Accept “seven days make one week”.
Thursday. Check that the day is written with a capital letter.
A-M-A-L. Accept the four letters said or written in the right order.
Leo. He likes football and mangoes. Award the mark for the name plus at least one thing that he likes.
Saturday. The story says that Saturday is the first day of the week at Amal's school.
Leo: June. Amal: December. Both months are needed for the mark.
She welcomed Nora and asked her to be her partner, because she remembered Leo's kindness on her own first day.
Shy at first, and brave by Thursday. Look for one piece of evidence from the story, such as the small voice or the welcome that she gives Nora.
Any kind action, such as saying hello, asking the child's name, offering to be a partner or sharing a book. The second mark is for saying how it would help.
Any day of the week with one real activity. Award the second mark when the day is spelled correctly with a capital letter.
The word that people call you. The other options define partner, calendar and like.
spell. The sentence asks for the letters of a name in order.
partner. Point back to Leo, who becomes Amal's partner in the story.
She. Nora is one girl, so he, they and we are all wrong here.
likes. Leo is one person, so the verb takes the s.
1. The day of writing, with a capital letter. 2. Any month, with a capital letter. 3. The child's own first school day; in the story it is Saturday. 4. Twelve.
Four sentences in two pairs. Check that the name comes first and that he or she then stays the same for that person, as in “This is Leo. He likes football.”
Three pairs of sentences. The spoken answer keeps like (“I like tea.”) and the report adds the s (“My mother likes tea.”).
Six questions with six matching answers: How do you spell …? answered with letters, What colour is …? answered with a colour, and How many … are there? answered with a number.
Listen for the full question, “What is your name? How do you spell it?”, and for the letters said one at a time with a small gap between them.
All seven days and all twelve months in order, with no repeats, followed by today's date in a full sentence.
A greeting, the child's own name, a question to the new child and a kind offer. Mark the warmth of the welcome as well as the words.
Four sentences naming a day, an order word, a month and a reason. Model: My special date is Saturday, the third of August. It is my birthday.
A greeting by name, an introduction, one thing the writer likes, one question with a question mark and one kind offer.
Six session sentences in order plus one goal sentence. Check that each sentence names something that really happened in that session.
Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, which is the order sung in the story.
January; June; September; November; then the child's own birthday month.
Three colour sentences and three number sentences, such as “The cups are white.” and “I can see four cups.”
Thursday, because the song sings Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday in that order.
12, the number of months on the calendar.
third, the order word. Three is the counting number.
She asked Nora to be her partner, just as Leo had done for her on Saturday.
What colour is your bag? It follows the pattern What colour is …? and ends with a question mark.
